title: Gov’t to Take Full Control of Angkor Wat, Sokimex Pushed Out - Khmer Times
highlights:
Angkor Wat, Sokimex Pushed Out
Sum Manet /  ...    The government will take full control of the iconic Angkor Wat temple complex from Sokimex Group, which had been authorized to manage the complex for more than 20 years, Prime Minister Hun Sen announced this morning in a post on his  ...    Sokimex received a concession to manage the temple complex in April 1999. The conglomerate, which runs fuel stations and hotels, is owned by tycoon Sok Kong. The controversial concession allowed his company to collect fees from ticket sales and share part of the proceeds with the government. The government will form a new public enterprise to manage Angkor Wat that will comprise officials from the Ministry of  ...    A report from the Apsara Authority, the government agency that manages the site, showed revenue from ticket sales at Angkor Wat rose 0.62 percent in the first 10 months of the year, to slightly more than $47 million. title: Sokimex and Government revisit Angkor deal | The Phnom Penh Post
highlights:
Cambodian Government has hammered out a new agreement with private company Sokimex over the ticket sales to the famed Angkor temples near Siem Reap. The agreement, reached at an interministerial meeting on Aug 15, may bring higher revenue to the Government. A Government official says Sokimex agreed to hand over 50 percent of its income if ticket sales to the Angkor park are lower than $3 million,  ...    temple complex, Cambodia's biggest tourism draw, last year. Under the deal signed in April 1999 Sokimex was to pay the Government a flat fee of $1 million in return for the ticket concession and could keep all revenue above that. The deal came after  ... title: Tickets, please. Government takes charge at Angkor Wat
highlights: Jan 12, 2016  ...    Since 1999, Sokimex, through its hospitality arm Sokha Hotels and Resorts, which operates hotels in Siem Reap, Sihanoukville and Phnom Penh, has sold tickets under a profit-sharing system with Apsara, led by deputy prime minister Sok An. The Cambodian government has officially taken charge of the ticket sales at the Angkor Archeological Park. In November prime minster Hun Sen announced an end to the Sok Kong-owned Sokimex’s control of the service. title: Cambodian state agency takes over ticket duties at Angkor Wat News
highlights: month. The government had terminated the contract of the previous privately owned ticketing agent at the end of last year. The Sokimex Group had held the concession to collect the entry fees at the UNESCO listed temples since April 1999. The agreement was terminated following vociferous complaints that contracting entry fee duties out to a private firm did not benefit the Cambodian people. Business magnate Oknha Sok Kong owns Sokimex. Among its diverse holdings, Sokimex has interests in luxury hotels and petrol stations. Revenue from ticket sales to Cambodia’s number one tourism draw is not small change and topped US$60 million last year. APSARA
